UserProfile.title: UserProfile
UserProfile.description: Represents the profile information for a business user, typically linked to an authentication system's user ID.

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserProfile entity. This typically corresponds to the authenticated user's ID (UID).

businessName.description: The registered name of the small business associated with this user profile.

contactEmail.description: The primary contact email address for the business.

contactPhone.description: The primary contact phone number for the business.

address.description: The physical address of the business.

currency.description: The default currency code (e.g., 'USD', 'EUR') used for financial tracking within the application.

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the user profile was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when the user profile was last updated.

Revenue.title: Revenue
Revenue.description: Records individual revenue entries for a business, contributing to overall financial tracking.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Revenue entity.

userId.description: Reference to the UserProfile that owns this revenue entry. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N Revenue)

amount.description: The monetary value of the revenue item.

description.description: A brief description of what this revenue item is for.

date.description: The date when the revenue was received or recorded.

source.description: The origin or type of revenue (e.g., 'Sales', 'Service Fee', 'Subscription').

isRecurring.description: Indicates if this revenue item is part of a recurring income stream.

paymentMethod.description: The method by which the revenue was received (e.g., 'Credit Card', 'Bank Transfer', 'Cash').

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the revenue entry was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when the revenue entry was last updated.

Expense.title: Expense
Expense.description: Records individual expense entries for a business, enabling detailed cost tracking.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Expense entity.

userId.description: Reference to the UserProfile that incurred this expense. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N Expense)

amount.description: The monetary value of the expense item.

description.description: A brief description of what this expense item is for.

date.description: The date when the expense occurred or was recorded.

isRecurring.description: Indicates if this expense item is part of a recurring cost.

paymentMethod.description: The method by which the expense was paid (e.g., 'Credit Card', 'Bank Transfer', 'Cash').

receiptUrl.description: An optional URL to a scanned receipt or invoice for the expense.

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the expense entry was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when the expense entry was last updated.

Appointment.title: Appointment
Appointment.description: Manages scheduled meetings or events within the business calendar.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Appointment entity.

userId.description: Reference to the UserProfile that scheduled this appointment. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N Appointment)

title.description: A concise title for the appointment.

description.description: A detailed description of the appointment's purpose or agenda.

startTime.description: The scheduled start date and time of the appointment.

endTime.description: The scheduled end date and time of the appointment.

location.description: The physical or virtual location where the appointment will take place.

status.description: The current status of the appointment (e.g., 'Scheduled', 'Confirmed', 'Completed', 'Cancelled').

reminderSent.description: A flag indicating whether an automated reminder for this appointment has been sent.

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the appointment entry was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when the appointment entry was last updated.

definition.description: Stores individual business user profiles. The document ID matches the authenticated user's ID (`userId`). Includes an `id` field which must match `userId` for clear ownership and authorization independence.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the authenticated user, corresponding to Firebase Authentication UID.

definition.description: Stores individual revenue entries for a specific user. Includes a denormalized `userId` field which must match the parent `userId` in the path and `request.auth.uid` for robust authorization independence.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user who owns this revenue entry.

params.name: revenueId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific revenue entry.

definition.description: Stores individual expense entries for a specific user. Includes a denormalized `userId` field which must match the parent `userId` in the path and `request.auth.uid` for robust authorization independence.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user who incurred this expense.

params.name: expenseId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific expense entry.

definition.description: Stores individual appointment entries for a specific user. Includes a denormalized `userId` field which must match the parent `userId` in the path and `request.auth.uid` for robust authorization independence.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user who scheduled this appointment.

params.name: appointmentId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific appointment entry.

firestore.reasoning:
The recommended Firestore structure for BizBoard prioritizes strong user ownership, authorization independence, and debuggability. All user-specific data is housed within subcollections directly under a user's profile document, following the pattern `/users/{userId}/<subcollectionName>/{docId}`. This design adheres to the core principles:

1.  **Authorization Independence (CRITICAL):** Each document in a subcollection (e.g., `/users/{userId}/revenues/{revenueId}`) will contain a denormalized `userId` field. This `userId` field *must* match the `{userId}` wildcard in its path and also `request.auth.uid`. This allows security rules to evaluate ownership and access permissions directly from the document itself and its path, without needing expensive and non-atomic `get()` operations on parent documents. For instance, a rule can simply check `allow read, write: if request.auth.uid == userId && resource.data.userId == userId;`. This makes atomic operations (like creating a user profile and their first revenue entry in a single batch) inherently secure and debuggable.

2.  **QAPs (Rules are not Filters):** By enforcing path-based ownership (e.g., all documents in `/users/{userId}/revenues` belong to `{userId}`), `list` operations are inherently secure and efficient. When a user requests to list documents from `/users/5l56WagvKGXxqnUJ5t4oBlnJSM73/revenues`, the security rules can check `request.auth.uid == '5l56WagvKGXxqnUJ5t4oBlnJSM73'`, ensuring that only the owner can list their own revenues. There's no need for client-side filtering or complex rule logic to restrict what documents are returned, guaranteeing that only authorized data is ever returned by a query. The error reported (`path: "/databases/(default)/documents/users/5l56WagvKGXxqnUJ5t4oBlnJSM73/routes"`) suggests the client code is attempting to query a collection named `routes`. Based on the provided entities, there is no `Route` entity. The client code should be updated to query the appropriate collections like `revenues`, `expenses`, or `appointments` under the user's path.

3.  **Structural Segregation:** Each subcollection (`revenues`, `expenses`, `appointments`) stores a homogeneous set of documents with identical security requirements (private to the user). This avoids mixing data with different access patterns in a single collection, simplifying security rules significantly.

4.  **DBAC (Database-Based Access Control):** All authorization is handled by checking `request.auth.uid` against the path wildcards and the denormalized `userId` field within the documents. No custom claims or server-side logic are required for basic access control, aligning with Firebase's best practices.

This structure provides a clear, scalable, and highly secure foundation for the BizBoard application, making security rules straightforward to write, test, and maintain.
